data.title: Celsius: Stronger Ties With Pepsi (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I rate Celsius Holdings (CELH) as a buy, as the company is reinforcing its partnership with Pepsi (PEP) gradually, so I would be more of "accumulate with caution," as there is still pending evidence to reinforce a stronger buy rating with higher conviction. Celsius has taken very important steps since my last article, such as the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar, but what gives me more confidence is the changes associated with the role of Pepsi in Celsius' distribution. Even when I think that it might cause some volatility in Celsius' stock performance in the next quarters, I am sure it's the right decision from a long-term perspective while avoiding repeating the problems related to the destocking of inventories in 2023 and 2024. Let's explore it.

Context

In Q3 2025, Celsius delivered good results in general; revenue growth was 172.9% YoY, reaching USD 725 million, beating expectations; gross margins also showed a substantial improvement from 46% to 51%, but net income was negative, -USD 61, as a result of the distribution termination fees:

This expense of USD 246 million is most likely related to the shift from Congo Brands distribution, the former owner of Alani Nu, to Pepsi's distribution, in which there would be a prior contractual agreement between Congo Brands and Alani Nu. I would expect that those distribution termination fees are a one-off.

On the other hand, adjusted diluted EPS was USD 0.42 per share, surpassing analyst consensus. Of course GAAP EPS was USD -0.27 per share because of the termination fees I explained above.

Now, 9-month revenue reached USD 1,793 million, which represents an annual growth of 75% YoY, whereas 9-month net income experienced -49.23% YoY, mostly influenced by the termination fees I previously mentioned.

Guidance for FY 2025

Celsius expects a full-year revenue of USD 3.76-3.79 billion, which would mean a strong revenue growth of 178% YoY. GAAP EPS (full year) is expected to reach between USD -1.05 and -0.75 per share and adjusted EPS (non-GAAP) between USD 6.10 and 6.40 per share.

This wide gap between GAAP EPS and non-GAAP EPS shows that management expects significant non-cash expenses, such as amortization and depreciation, and integration-related charges, such as restructuring costs mainly associated with the new Pepsi captaincy as part of a new distribution agreement with Celsius, which involves the recent Celsius acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar.

Celsius acquires Alani Nu and Rockstar

There is no doubt that Alani Nu and Rockstar (US and Canada assets) will open new growth avenues for Celsius, particularly Alani Nu, given its size and huge growth (114% YoY in Q3), while holding a solid market share of 7.2% dollar share of the US ready-to-drink energy category.

In February 2025, Celsius acquired Alani Nu, which is a female-focused health & wellness beverage brand, paying USD 1.8 million, including taxes. This is a very good acquisition, as it will enable Celsius to grow in new markets.

If we take the revenues of Alani Nu and Celsius from FY2024, we would see that Alani Nu would represent around 44% of Celsius's total sales, which is a meaningful acquisition.

On the other hand, the other Celsius acquisition is the Rockstar's assets from the US and Canada. Rockstar holds a market share of 2.4% dollar share of the US ready-to-drink energy category. Rockstar experienced a decline of 9% YoY in Q3 2025, which I think is a temporary setback as Pepsi and Celsius finish their migration of systems and logistics under the new Pepsi's expanded responsibility in the distribution of Celsius products.

Indeed, these new acquisitions mean not only two new subsidiaries within Celsius but also a new distribution platform and operations under Pepsi's distribution, the new Pepsi's captaincy.

Celsius-Pepsi vs. Monster-Coke: Unequal Energy Distribution Play

This new arrangement in 2025 between Pepsi and Celsius is a material change with respect to my last article in November 2024, as I mentioned in it that there was a stronger commitment in the deal between Coca-Cola (KO) and Monster (MNST) than that of Pepsi and Celsius, so my perception then was that Pepsi's support to Celsius needed to be stronger for triggering a better Celsius performance over the long haul.

In this sense, one of the key aspects I noticed in my last article was that Coca-Cola owns Monster's common shares and Pepsi owns Celsius' preferred shares that pay annual dividends of 5%. It has important implications about how those investments are registered in Coca-Cola's and Pepsi's financial statements, respectively, which might trigger different incentives for each other to support their respective investees.

Indeed, Pepsi's investment in Celsius' preferred shares is registered as an available-for-sale asset (AFS) in Pepsi's balance sheet. Any change of the investment is registered in the accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI), which is an account registered within the equity in the balance sheet. Pepsi's net income will not be impacted by that investment, as any loss in that investment would be included in the AOCI in the equity of Pepsi's balance sheet. Pepsi's ROE and ROIC might even be pushed up as Pepsi's equity would reduce if Celsius' preferred shares go south.

Conversely, Coca-Cola registered its investment in Monster's common shares using the equity method, which means that Monster's revenues and net income are included in Coca-Cola's income statements. In this sense, I stated in my last article that this kind of investment, through common shares, represented a way better alignment than that of Pepsi with Celsius, as Coca-Cola had the incentives that Monster performs very well to show better combined revenues and net income.

This evidence showed me that Pepsi did not consider its investment in Celsius as strategic as that of Monster with Coca-Cola when I assessed Celsius in November 2024. Nevertheless, now there are clear steps toward a stronger partnership that may support Celsius' long-term performance.

Pepsi's captaincy: a gradual solution to Celsius's destocking problem and a stronger relationship

The beginning of a stronger relationship between Pepsi and Celsius might have started as a result of the serious problems associated with the destocking of inventories in 2023 and 2024, and both partners might have agreed about a new partnership strategy during the acquisition of Rockstar, as I saw the next table in the Celsius 10Q for the Q3 FY 2025:

Indeed, in the table above, it's shown that there was an exchange of assets between Pepsi and Celsius; Celsius issued preferred shares for USD 935.7 million to Pepsi, whereas Pepsi sold Rockstar's assets to Celsius for USD 307 million in a non-cash operation.

Of those USD 935.7 million, only USD 585 million are convertible preferred shares into common shares, which would increase Pepsi's current stake from 8.5% to around 11% in Celsius if Pepsi converts them into common shares on a given future date. This is a higher commitment between Pepsi and Celsius, even if those shares are still preferred.

In this sense, under these exchanges, Celsius issued preferred shares of USD 937.7 million, and Pepsi sold the Rockstar assets from the US and Canada for USD 307 million and the working capital of USD 29 million, so there is a difference of USD 598.7 million in favor of Pepsi, which is registered as a non-cash upfront payment to Pepsi (black circle); in other words, Celsius is paying Pepsi, with preferred shares, for captaincy for distribution of the brands Celsius, Rockstar, and Alani Nu.

In my view, this is critical to understand since this is what separates the Celsius of 2025 from the Celsius before 2025.

In Celsius' 10Q, I've found the following about Pepsi's captaincy:

Under the Captaincy, Pepsi is obligated to use commercially reasonable efforts to sell and distribute the Company’s energy drink portfolio in the U.S. and to prioritize the Company's products within its beverage distribution system. The arrangement provides the Company with enhanced control and oversight of the energy drink category within Pepsi’s U.S. distribution network, including the ability to determine product facings, merchandising allocations and certain promotional priorities for energy beverages.

This is a total expansion of responsibilities for Pepsi, since it implies that Pepsi will manage the inventory planning involvement, the demand forecasting, and the retail execution through a unified portfolio management, which includes Celsius, Rockstar, and Alani Nu. In other words, Pepsi has incentive alignment, controlling the retail execution more deeply, unifying data across all retailers, and centralizing a prior fragmented distribution.

Before 2025, Pepsi's job was simply to execute delivery, not design strategy; indeed, I haven't found anything about the word "captaincy" in any of the 10Ks and 10Qs before 2025. It means that, in previous years, Celsius was mainly responsible for its retail execution strategy, product planning, SKU prioritization, and shelf management with clients.

Now, you can understand that that was the main factor that caused the destocking problem in 2024, as there was a serious misalignment between Pepsi and Celsius. Indeed, Pepsi bottlers aggressively loaded channels because Celsius was growing exponentially, bottlers were incentivized by volume, and Pepsi did not control upstream planning, only downstream sales.

As such, these changes in Pepsi's expanded responsibility, the increase of the potential stake in Pepsi to 11% of Celsius, and the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar give me more confidence about Celsius' prospects. However, there are still some challenges in the short and middle term.

Celsius will require a robust share buyback program through 2031 and flawless execution of its partnership with Pepsi

After the agreement of Pepsi's captaincy in the distribution, the automatic conversion date of Pepsi's preferred shares was extended to 2031, with that conversion happening automatically if certain conditions are met.

Now, it's important to know how many shares Celsius will need to issue in 2031 if Pepsi's preferred shares are converted into new common shares, which will dilute current shareholders. Celsius reported that 22 million common shares are required for the Series A preferred shares and 11.3 million common shares to satisfy the Series B preferred shares, so 33 million common shares will be issued in 2031 if they are automatically converted.

Assuming an average Celsius stock price of USD 50 per share, that would mean that Celsius needs to repurchase around USD 1.6 billion to mitigate dilution among its current investors. The good news is that Celsius announced on November 10, 2025, a new repurchase program of USD 300 million, preparing the path for the future conversion of the preferred shares held by Pepsi. If Celsius keeps spending USD 300 million per year until 2031, most of the potential dilution as a result of the conversion of the preferred shares held by Pepsi to common shares will be mitigated.

On the other hand, related to the changes in Pepsi's responsibilities as part of its captaincy, this implies migrations of distributions and logistics, information transfer and data integrity, and supply chain disruptions, among other restrictions. In other words, this new change in the strategy of the distribution and planification is very good over the long haul for Celsius, but it will not be an easy path, which can create short-term setbacks that might impact Celsius's performance in the upcoming quarters.

So even when I upgraded to "buy" Celsius stock, it was more a "soft buy," which implies accumulation with caution to take advantage of the potential volatility of the stock in the next quarters.

Valuation

According to SA Quant ratings, the stock seems to be expensive, as the grade is D+, being more expensive than its group of comparables. However, we need to take into account that, most likely, none of its comparables has a revenue growth like that shown by Celsius.

Let's see a valuation method that enables us to have more control of the different assumptions. I will use the following assumptions:

  • Outstanding shares: 252.8 million (shares diluted as of September 2025)

  • FCF Margins: 10% (FCF margins in 2024 and 2023 were 17% and 9.3%, respectively, but it's a very volatile metric in Celsius)

  • I assume that Celsius is held until the year 2030.

  • Revenue growth from 2025 to 2026: according to consensus.

  • Revenue growth beyond 2026: my own expectations under a conservative scenario.

  • P/FCF 2027: 50x (optimistic assumption)

  • Discount rate: 10% (given the changes in the strategy toward more predictability for the long haul)

Based on our assumptions, I projected the revenues until 2030; I was very conservative in the years 2028, 2029, and 2030, as it's supposed that Pepsi's distribution will be very well established in those years after all the changes started in 2025 as part of the new partnership agreement. I was conservative, assuming a growth of 10% in those 3 last years, reaching a revenue of USD 4.84 billion for 2030. I multiply those revenues by my assumption of 10% FCF margins to get an estimated FCF for 2030 of USD 484.6 million.

Now, I take that FCF of USD 484.6 million to be divided by the outstanding number of shares of 252.8 million to get an FCF per share for 2030, getting 1.92. In this sense, I take the FCF per share of 1.92 and multiply it by 50x, which is closer to the lowest end of a range between 38x and 129x in the last 4 years.

Then, I get a target price of USD 95.9 per share in 2030, so I calculate the present value to bring it back to 2025.

Thus, finding the intrinsic value:

Intrinsic value = 95.9/(1+discounted rate)^5

Finally, I get an approximate intrinsic value of USD 54 per share, which would leave us a potential upside of 38% considering the current stock price. Even with that upside, I take a "soft" buy or accumulate with caution, as there could still be some logistic challenges related to the migration to Pepsi's distribution in Alani Nu's case and Pepsi's expanded responsibility with the Celsius brand.

On the other hand, under a strong execution by the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius, the intrinsic value can be way higher, but I take a conservative stance as Celsius and Pepsi need to prove a smooth transition to the new strategy implemented.

As a result, there could be some volatility in Celsius performance that might cause higher volatility in Celsius' stock price. Long-term investors like to take advantage of the ups and downs of the stock price to accumulate shares gradually, reflecting the short-term adjustments in the Celsius strategy and knowing, at the same time, that there is a sound long-term strategy behind

Risks

There are two risks to my thesis: i) an outstanding execution of the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius making a very smooth transition to the new strategy, which can reduce the volatility in the inventories while strengthening Celsius' predictability, making my thesis too conservative, and ii) a very poor execution of the partnership between Pepsi and Celsius causing so much volatility in the inventories, repeating a similar problem like the destocking in 2024, combined with high integration costs, pushing down the stock price as a result of the lack of confidence by the market in a smooth transition combined with poor execution.

So, my thesis takes into account both scenarios: accumulate with caution in case things go south and you are not fully invested, and, at the same time, accumulate with caution in case the company delivers better than expected. Behind that thesis is the fact that there have been strong advances in the partnership between Celsius and Pepsi, which aligns their goals better.

In the upcoming years, and looking into the table above, I would expect better returns on capital and more consistent high revenue growth combined with consistently high FCF margins. I think that we'll see that as Celsius keeps taking rational decisions, but we expect those decisions to be well executed.

Takeaways

Celsius has strengthened its relationship with Pepsi through a new arrangement that provides Pepsi with enhanced control and oversight of Celsius' energy drink portfolio within Pepsi’s U.S. distribution network. This new arrangement, combined with Pepsi's higher participation in Celsius with a new potential of an 11% stake and the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar, changes the picture I had, which made me upgrade the stock to a "buy," or more an "accumulation with caution."

In addition, in my previous article, I mentioned that Pepsi would convert its preferred shares into common shares in 2028 if certain conditions are met, which were not disclosed at that time. Now, in 2025, there are some changes, as the option to convert them is still 2028, giving other opportunities for Pepsi to make the conversion automatically in 2031 and disclosing the conditions to be met for that conversion.

All this shows me that Pepsi is more involved in its investment in Celsius while advancing in its commitment. Although the relationship between Pepsi and Celsius is not as strong as that of Coca-Cola and Monster yet, there is a clear advancement toward that level of commitment, and that is a material change with respect to my previous article.

data.title: PLTY: The Easy Money Was Already Made (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

After covering a plethora of different YieldMax funds, I've been able to closely observe how these funds perform through different market cycles. YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (PLTY) is currently one of the highest yielding ETFs within their suite, which makes it a popular choice for income investors. When I previously covered PLTY, I issued a buy rating due to the positive momentum of Palantir (PLTR) and its ability to achieve house money status. Since then, PLTY has provided a total return above 68% when including all distributions paid.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PLTY's share price has declined by about 19.61%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 96.77% over the same time frame. The main appeal of PLTY is the massive starting dividend yield of more than 125%. Since the time of my last coverage, PLTY has shifted its payout frequency to a weekly cadence and makes it an attractive choice for investors seeking a high rate of income generation. However, new payouts are declared every week, so the listed dividend yield is likely to be different based on the time you read this.

Since my last coverage, the tech markets have experienced heightened volatility. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's performance, use case, and risk profile as we approach the new year. I believe that PLTY's performance has been so strong due to the positive momentum of the underlying. However, historical data amongst similar YieldMax peers indicates that the downside risk could be massive if sentiment across the tech and AI sectors starts to shift. This could result in rapid capital deterioration and declining payouts over time. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that PLTY implements to generate the large income it needs to support payouts.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PLTY has total net assets of $627M and has a gross expense ratio of 0.99%. The fund aims to generate weekly income by selling call options or call spreads on Palantir. The caveat is that PLTY implements a synthetic option writing strategy. The term synthetic means that PLTY doesn't actually own any underlying common shares of Palantir, which means that the fund is writing options against an asset that it doesn't actually own. This can increase the risk profile in terms of price volatility and limited growth potential.

As we can see from the holdings below, PLTY is backed by U.S Treasuries. We can also confirm that PLTY doesn't hold any common shares of PLTR. However, we can see several call options that are written with varying expiration dates. For instance, the largest call option is written with a strike price of $180 per share with an expiration date of January 16th of 2026.

  • PLTR US - 01/16/2026 C$180
  • PLTR US - 01/16/2026 C$200

If you aren't familiar with the process, the inclusion of an option writing strategy means that PLTY will only be able to partially participate in the upside movement of Palantir. This is because the upside growth is capped at whatever the selected strike price is, which is $180 and $200 per share in this instance. So, for example, if PLTY sees its price run up to $210 per share before the expiration date, PLTY would only be able to capture the price growth up to the $200 strike price.

This means that the remaining upside growth is sacrificed for the income generation that can be collected now from the option premiums. Since PLTR is a volatile stock, the implied volatility translates to high option premiums that can be collected. Therefore, a position in PLTY only makes sense for investors that are specifically seeking income generation.

Performance Insights

Looking at the fund's performance since inception, we can see that PLTY's share price has slightly increased by 5.78%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 182% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time. However, the total return is entirely dependent on when an investor initiated their starting position. Therefore, timing is very important with a fund like PLTY.

Despite the recent pullback in its share price, Palantir still trades near its all-time high. At the time of my last coverage, I initiated a buy rating due to the market's overall pullback during April. However, I believe that the overall appeal right now is a bit less attractive as markets continue to rise upwards. After close observation of PLTY and other YieldMax funds, I believe that the performance can be lumped into three different scenarios.

  • Palantir's Price Rises = PLTY is likely to see minimal upside, and distributions will remain consistent. However, larger distributions may actually cause the share price to remain flat, rather than show any growth, since the fund is paying out most of its gains.
  • Palantir Trades Flat/Choppy = PLTY's share price may slowly trend downward, but distributions may be high due to volatility. This can lead to PLTY outperforming underlying Palantir shares during a period of choppiness and may present the most favorable conditions for PLTY.
  • Palantir's Price Falls = PLTY will suffer from share price erosion, and payouts may trend downward, depending on how severe of a downside movement takes place. PLTY will struggle to recover from this downside, and underperformance is likely to deepen.

Based on this logic, I believe that PLTY's favorable performance is most likely attributed to the fact that PLTR has maintained consistent and rapid growth for the year. Once momentum eventually slows, I anticipate that PLTY's share price is likely to experience erosion over time. In a scenario where volatility slows, PLTY may find itself in a situation where it is paying out more than it actually earns in income.

If we compare the performance of PLTY versus PLTR, we can see that there are discrepancies. Although PLTY has provided a favorable total return of 182%, the fund still severely underperforms PLTR's total return of 352.4% over the same time frame. So if you're bullish on the underlying company, it would be better to simply own the common shares of PLTR.

However, I believe that the most important aspect of the performance chart above is the price change of PLTY following the market's pullback in April earlier this year. PLTR pulled back alongside the market in response to President Trump's tariff proposals. Eventually the market and PLTR recovered over the remainder of the year. However, not only did PLTY miss out on this growth, it failed to even recover back to its levels prior to the decline. Therefore, I worry that PLTY's share price will experience rapid erosion if another downturn takes place. Since PLTY's inception, the fund hasn't really experienced a strong downturn in PLTR's share price.

While the upside growth of PLTY is capped by the inclusion of the option writing strategy, the downside risk is not. While it's certainly possible that the distributions can offset any short-term losses, the reality is that large distributions being paid out during a period of declines can exacerbate the price decay that occurs. For this reason, I remain cautious about adding here because sentiment in the tech and AI sector changes rapidly. PLTR already trades at a massive price-to-earnings ratio, and if optimism within the AI sector fades, PLTR can experience a strong pullback.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.5631 per share, the current dividend yield sits slightly above 125.4%. New distributions are declared every Wednesday, and payouts are made every Friday. Since new distribution amounts are always being declared, the listed dividend yield will consistently shift week to week. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that payouts are highly variable. The payout amounts are determined by the success of written options, implied volatility of PLTR, and overall momentum of the market. Therefore, PLTY may not be the best choice for investors that want to prioritize consistent distributions over a long period of time.

As we've already mentioned, the fund has a limited operating history. Therefore, investors haven't yet experienced a major cut to payouts over its operating window so far. In a scenario where the share price declines, PLTY will likely need to adjust payouts downward to offset a declining NAV. With an annual dividend yield above 125%, the fund may find itself paying out more than it actually earns sometimes.

However, the biggest strength of this large yield is the potential to achieve house money status. I refer to this as the cross-point where an investor has received dividends that repay their initial investment amount. So if you invest $10,000 in PLTY, it is technically possible to collect all of your initial investment back in the form of distributions within a year.

$10,000 invested = ~$12,500 in dividends received over 12 months.

Of course, this assumes that payouts and the estimated yield remains the same. The actual time it takes to achieve house money status can take longer or shorter than a year, based on the underlying movement of PLTR. Once an investor reaches house money status, the overall risk profile is drastically reduced since you've repaid your initial investment, but still own the shares that continue to produce income week after week.

Additionally, PLTY has the potential to issue distributions in a tax-efficient manner. According to the latest distribution breakdown, the last payout contained 92.53% of return of capital. Unlike traditional distributions, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and, therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale.

However, the makeup of the distributions can change every week. Some weeks may contain a lot of return of capital, while other weeks contain no return of capital. So although PLTY has the potential to pay out tax-efficient distributions, this doesn't mean that the income received is guaranteed to be tax free. For this reason, I would still suggest utilizing PLTY within a tax-advantaged account to ensure that the tax consequences are limited.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on PLTY to a hold at this time. PLTY's performance has remained strong over the last year, but this is due to the fact that the common shares of Palantir have continued to rise on a consistent basis. However, investors shouldn't confuse this exceptional performance with PLTY's ability to provide favorable returns. I believe that PLTY is still extremely vulnerable to a scenario where PLTR faces a rapid pullback. The combination of the large distributions and capped upside growth means that PLTY is very unlikely to recover from a downturn.

data.title: Equitable Holdings: Business Transformation Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Shares of Equitable Holdings (EQH) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining just about 4%. While the company continues to aggressively repurchase stock and transformed its business with a large reinsurance transaction, mixed results throughout the year have dampened enthusiasm and weighed on shares. I last covered shares in September when I rated the stock a "buy," but since then, performance has been disappointing, with the stock down 8%. With updated financials and other material developments, now is a good time to revisit EQH to determine if performance can reaccelerate in 2026. I view it as a top pick for the next year.

In the company's third quarter, reported on November 4th, EQH earned $1.67, which beat expectations by $0.06, a welcome development after mixed results in the first half of the year that raised questions about underwriting quality. Assets under management are now $1.1 trillion, up 4% sequentially and 7% from last year. Wealth management fees rose by 7% to $197 million, while AllianceBernstein revenue grew 6% to $821 million. Retirement revenue was up 4%, thanks to ongoing strength in RILA sales. As you can see below, its retirement insurance business still drives the majority of earnings, but its fee-based businesses are now about a third of profitability. I expect this share to grow over time.

As part of this effort to increase fee-based revenue, in late October, Equitable announced a deal to acquire 110 advisors and $9 billion in client assets from Stifel (SF). Diversifying away from capital-intensive, regulated insurance businesses and to the capital-light, fee-centric asset and wealth management has been a core focus in recent years, and this deal expands upon that effort. EQH has about $118 billion of assets in its wealth management unit, so this deal is more bolt-on in nature and is not transformative by any means. Still, I view the strategic push into recurring cash-flow businesses as a long-term positive for its valuation. This deal should close in early 2026.

Its wealth management unit continues to perform very well. Wealth management advisory inflows were $2.2 billion with an 11% organic growth rate over the past year. In fact, earnings are essentially already at the 2027 target. While improved organic growth is a meaningful tailwind, EQH has also been a beneficiary of the strong market rally. Advisory fees are charged as a share of AUM, so as the market goes up, revenue and earnings mechanistically rise as well. With the market sitting near an all-time high, we should see further revenue and earnings growth over the next few quarters.

Elsewhere, AllianceBernstein continues to perform well. I would note that because of the RGA deal, some assets moved from AllianceBernstein to RGA. As a result, AB's had a $2.3 billion outflow; excluding this, inflows were $1.7 billion.

A key strategic priority of AB has been expanding its private market AUM, given the higher fees. As part of this, it has acquired new capabilities, buying businesses like CarVal and lifting teams from other firms. As a result, AUM has grown considerably and should be on track to surpass $90 billion by 2027. As part of this, EQH has invested $17 billion of its general account into private strategies, with $3 billion still committed.

Private assets, particularly pockets of private credit have seen increased scrutiny of late after several high-profile defaults in recent months. There are two risks here: 1) that fears reduce AUM growth for AB and 2) that EQH faces losses on its $17 billion portfolio. While management noted that some investors have taken more risk in the sector, contributing to recent losses, it has maintained the same underwriting standards. Recent credit performance has also been better than expected, and it has not been impacted by the high-profile losses announced by other firms. With AUM up 17% from last year, it also does not appear that recent headlines have materially altered investment demand.

Looking further at its investment portfolio, I was encouraged to see its alternatives portfolio earn 8% in the quarter. As you can see below, alternative returns have been relatively poor since 2021. There has been a modest rebound this year, but real estate continues to be a drag given the rate environment and structural pressures on commercial properties. While its long-term track record is 10%, that is only due to outsized returns in 2021. I continue to believe alternative returns are more likely to run at the low end of their 8-12% target.

It is also important to remember that its portfolio is 98% investment grade, and 70% of the portfolio is A-rated or higher. As a result, its portfolio has an average A2 rating, which is extremely high quality. While 20% of the portfolio is in mortgage, the loan-to-value here is just 67%, and debt service coverage is 1.9x. Both of these factors should keep defaults and ultimate losses to a minimum.

Turning to its insurance operation, retirement earnings were down to $399 million from $414 million. This was primarily due to higher commissions. Inflows have been extremely strong, at $6 billion of gross inflows in Q3, and this high level of sales increases commissions paid to agents. Over time, it earns back this commission with the fees and investment spread on the product, so in a sense, this is a "high-quality" earnings problem, and in my view, it is more of a timing issue than a run-rate earnings headwind.

Earlier this year, Equitable reinsured its individual life exposure with the Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), in a transformative deal that reduced its back book and freed up capital for buybacks, M&A, and organic growth. During the third quarter, EQH bought back $676 million of stock and paid down $500 million of debt. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down nearly 7% from last year.

Even after this, the company still has $800 million of cash at the holding company, in excess of its $500 million target. There will also likely be a $400 million dividend to the holding company in Q4. This should enable capital returns to remain elevated. Plus, shares offer a 2.2% dividend yield, which I view as very secure.

Separately, during the third quarter, Equitable completed its annual actuarial review. Insurers periodically review how policies are performing relative to assumptions and adjust reserves accordingly. As a result of this, EQH reported a -$63 million development, setting aside more reserves to reflect underperformance. This is not surprising to me, given the negative developments in H1. Mortality continues to be worse than expected, though much of this risk has now been reinsured, reducing the sensitivity of results. I view this as a manageable risk as a result.

Back in September, I was targeting $1.65 billion of free cash flow this year, with the total likely to reach $2 billion by 2027. I expect EQH to come within $25 million of my $1.65 billion target this year. Beyond this year, I expect about $4.25 in capital returns per share next year, rising past $4.75 in 2027, given ongoing inflows into its asset management platforms and solid annuity sales.

Generally speaking, I believe fee-based businesses like wealth and asset management should trade closer to 20x and annuity-oriented insurance businesses 10x capital returns. That points to shares being worth $55 today and closer to $60 by 2027 at ~13x capital returns given its current business mix. However, with this growth largely coming from fee-based businesses, its multiple should improve over time. This is why I see shares getting closer to $65 over the next 12-18 months. With about 30% upside, I am upgrading shares to a "strong buy" as markets are underestimating its improving business mix.

data.title: Gold: 1% To All-Time Highs, Getting Jealous Of Silver
data.description:
By Elior Manier

As highlighted in our overnight session rewind, gold has quickly breached above $4,300 and is now racing towards new all-time highs. The Fed's cutting cycle and overall 2025 US exceptionalism from the Trump Administration have had a considerable effect on the demand for non-fiat assets, and gold is the primary beneficiary of such demand.

The financial world order since the early 2000s has been characterized by high demand for US Treasuries. As the US maintained higher rates relative to others, even during the Global Financial Crisis, and consequently recovered much more solidly than its OECD peers, it absorbed the flows from the entire globe.

Metals, on the other hand, were getting replaced by their yielding rivals - Treasuries. Some countries, like Canada, have emptied their gold reserves, for example, making questionable decisions.

However, as yields had been trending lower, particularly after the QE, demand for metals reformed again, and now, their attraction is without question.

Government deficits are ever-increasing, even with a stable global economy, and the US seemingly cannot be as trustful of a global riches reserve, given the several diplomatic heatwaves provided by Donald Trump.

Tariffs aren't the most welcomed policies if you want to retain buyers of your government bonds. Particularly when you're cutting rates. In any case, since reaching its COVID lows in March 2020 ($1,451), gold is up close to 200%, and most of its gains have occurred since February 2024.

However, what is grabbing markets' attention is how strong the acceleration has been ongoing since August 2025 and Powell's Jackson Hole speech, which may have been a turning point for global markets.

From August: Silver (XAG) and other metals in focus as the Federal Reserve's independence gets challenged – silver was trading at $38 at that time.

Let's dive into a multi-timeframe gold analysis to get a closer look at the post-FOMC rebound as the yellow metal aims to protect its throne.

Gold (XAU/USD) Multi-Timeframe Analysis, Technical Levels, and Potential Price Targets

Daily Chart

Our pre-FOMC metals analysis pointed to a potential breakout in gold after a triangle consolidation – and it is currently playing out. Bulls used the 50-day moving average as support. Keep a close eye on it, as it has been serving as loyal support throughout 2025. The rest will be to see if buyers can make the push beyond new highs.

Up 3.75% in 3 sessions, momentum is gathering some heat despite some not-so-dovish 2026 Fed cut projections - until more data is served for markets (Tuesday 16 - US NFP), not much can come to stop the rally.

Metal buyers just wanted to see rates coming down, and they are getting served. Silver and its ongoing frenzy is dragging demand for such commodities higher.

An Interesting Chart: Silver to Gold Ratio

4H Chart, Technical Levels and Potential Price targets

As indicated in our recent piece (link just above), a measured move higher (yellow squares) could take prices anywhere from $4,500 to $4,575 if buyers manage to break recent highs.

Levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD) trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Current all-time high resistance: $4,300 to $4,400
  • $4,380: Current all-time highs
  • Fib-induced potential new ATH resistance: $4,500 to $4,575
  • Session highs: $4,346 (and counting)

Support Levels

  • Hourly Pivot and Triangle top: $4,200 to $4,240
  • 50-Day MA: $4,150
  • Major Pivot: $3,950 to $4,000 (200-period MA)
  • $3,700 consolidation Support
  • $3,500 Major Support

1H Chart

Despite the overbought conditions on all timeframes, the rally isn't showing signs of stopping. Watch for any stalling of momentum, however. A consolidation between $4,300 and $4,350 provides higher chances of a breakout as the RSI slows down. A retracement, however, points to a more balanced price action going forward.

Safe Trades!

data.title: Nike Earnings Preview: Expect A Better Quarter, But Elliott Is Still Running A Marathon With A Piano On His Back
data.description:
When Nike (NKE) reports their fiscal Q2 ’26 financial results after the bell on Thursday, December 18, 2025, consensus is expecting $12.22 billion in revenue, $696 million in operating income, and $0.38 in EPS, for expected year-over-year (y-o-y) growth of -1%, -50%, and -51%.

Jefferies put out a pretty bullish report this week on the company and the stock, and while this blog expects the Q2 ’26 numbers to be better, the full-fiscal year results, which aren't over until the May ’26 quarter is reported in June ’26, are likely to show 12-month revenue of +1%, full-year operating income down -15% and full-year EPS down 22% for the 12 months ended May ’26.

In other words, readers should expect the next few quarters to be “better,” but any dramatic turnaround is still a ways away.

In fiscal Q1 ’26, the good news was that it was the 2nd straight quarter of gains in the order book; revenue and EPS estimates were mostly unchanged after Q1 ’26 was reported, meaning that the downward revisions – at least for now – looked to have stopped, while the bad news was that the tariff impact was boosted from $1 billion to $1.5 billion and will have a 120 bp negative impact on FY ’26 gross margin.

North American EBIT (Nike still reports EBIT and not operating income) saw its third consecutive quarter of sequential improvement.

China is still 13% of total Nike revenue and 40% of EBIT; thus, it’s not an immaterial region for the shoe giant, but given the tariff environment, I wouldn’t expect great improvement in the regional metrics.

If Nike is going to start outperforming, it will likely be driven by North American results.

Valuation

When Nike peaked near $180 in late 2021, the stock was trading near 5x revenue, but today, assuming the quarterly revenue estimate is met, Nike is trading at 1.7x trailing twelve-month revenue.

Looking back at the historical spreadsheet, the last time Nike traded below 2x revenue was in 2014, per this blog’s numbers, so the stock is getting cheaper, but as the old saying goes, it’s all relative.

At 40x expected fiscal EPS of $1.68, Nike is still expected to show EPS down y-o-y 22%. The other metrics look similar to the PE: price to cash flow and price to free cash flow are 29x and 35x (ex-cash), respectively.

The only reasonably attractive metric is the dividend yield today at 2.42%, and even that doesn’t look great relative to money market yields.

Morningstar’s “intrinsic value” estimate is $104, which leaves the stock trading at a 35% discount to expected fair value, which is one of the reasons the stock is still held by clients today, since, in this market, it’s hard to find individual companies trading at that big of a discount to perceived intrinsic value.

One interesting aspect to the valuation story probably not understood by most readers is that Nike’s quality of earnings has actually improved, as cash flow and free cash flow now fully cover net income, although cash flow is down from its peak levels as well. Nike had a 3% free-cash-flow yield at the end of last quarter.

Summary/conclusion

My own opinion is that Elliott Hill deserves the benefit of the doubt in terms of restoring the Nike brand and returning the footwear and apparel giant to some semblance of mid-single-digit and low double-digit revenue and EPS growth over the next few years.

The SKINS initiative with Kim Kardashian just launched at the end of Q1 ’26, so perhaps that initiative will be updated on this call, while running, which grew 20% y-o-y last quarter, had some interesting things happening with its Austin, TX, store, but these are still small relative to the Nike whole; however, it shows that Elliott isn’t standing still.

The reader commentary anytime I post on Nike is usually addressing, “Nike went woke, and got broke” or something along those lines (which isn’t completely wrong), but the abrogation of the retail footwear space allowing the likes of Hoka, Skechers, and ON to grab easy market share from Nike over the last few years was just criminal in terms of destroying the brand.

These newer brands have the “it” factor that Nike used to have, and Elliott needs to reclaim that, and it’s not going to be easy.

In terms of portfolio construction, Nike is the kind of stock that if AI and the dominant growth companies hit a serious rough patch, the stock is completely uncorrelated or disconnected from the large-cap growth and “major brand” class and would likely hold its value in a difficult market since it’s still down more than 50% from its late 2021 high. (That’s one reason it’s still being kept in client accounts.)

Former articles on Nike can be found here, here, here, and here.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All EPS, revenue and cash flow estimate data sourced from LSEG.

Original Post

data.title: Cognyte Software: Margins Are Expanding As Backlog Builds
data.description:
As we near the end of 2025, I continue to emphasize the need for a portfolio rotation out of expensive large-cap growth stocks and into individual small- and mid-cap names that have less correlation to the broader market. We don't need to eliminate or reduce our exposure to growth, tech, AI, and all the other themes that moved the market this year, we just need to concentrate on less-obvious names that aren't at nosebleed multiples.

Cognyte Software Ltd. (CGNT), in my view, is a great candidate. The public sector AI company has only seen its share price rise ~10% this year, underperforming the S&P 500 while still enjoying all the same AI catalysts as the more prominent competitors in its field, namely Palantir (PLTR). Amid revenue growth and substantial margin expansion, accompanying a robust 3-year target, Cognyte remains a fantastic buying opportunity post-Q3 earnings.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Cognyte in October, when the stock was trading in the mid-$8s. Since then, Cognyte has delivered a significant Q3 beat-and-raise, with the company muscling past a federal government shutdown to deliver strong results. Cognyte has also begun to generate meaningful profit, which is fully supporting its valuation. To me, the post-earnings rally is just the beginning of a longer upward re-rating: I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this stock.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here are the core long-term bull case drivers for Cognyte:

  • Sticky, high-spending public sector customers. Cognyte deals directly with large public sector agencies in the United States, Asia, and Europe. Though it mostly sells via perpetual licenses, the company notes that customers tend to renew at a very high rate while also upselling to additional capacity and functionalities.
  • Wide array of use cases. Think of Cognyte as a big data/analytics platform in the same vein as Palantir. Its product spans a vast array of use cases, ranging from border protection, counternarcotics operations, military intelligence, financial crimes investigations, and anti-money laundering operations, among others.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Though not yet a Rule of 40 software business, the company balances a mid-teens revenue growth rate alongside a mid-teens adjusted EBITDA margin, on top of ongoing free cash flow and a clean, debt-free balance sheet.

Despite the AI angle in this stock, it still trades at modest multiples. At current share prices near $10, the stock trades at a market cap of $716.9 million. Netting off the $106.6 million of cash off the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $610.3 million.

For the remainder of FY26, Cognyte has boosted its full-year revenue to $400 million, or 14% y/y growth, versus $397 million (+13% y/y) previously. It's now expecting $47 million adjusted EBITDA, or a 12% margin, versus an 11% margin previously.

This positions Cognyte's valuation at 1.5x EV/FY26 revenue and 13.0x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. We note as well that in FY25, Cognyte has laid out an aggressive three-year plan to hit $500 million in revenue by FY28 (two years from now), at a >20% adjusted EBITDA margin. If we take management's targets at face value and assume $100 million in adjusted EBITDA in FY28, the stock today trades at just ~6x that earnings potential.

In other words, Cognyte is still an earlier-stage growth stock, trading at prices that haven't yet been fully discovered by the stock market. "Buy" here with confidence.

Q3 Download

Let's now go through Cognyte's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Cognyte's revenue increased 13% y/y to $100.7 million, which came in ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $99.5 million (+12% y/y) by a one-point margin.

The company continued to build up backlog, up 10% y/y to $358.9 million in short-term RPO (which will be recognized as revenue within the next twelve months). We do note that one of the watch items for the quarter is a slowdown in the company's pace of billings, which grew only 3% y/y to $107.7 million after a 22% y/y growth pace in Q2.

Ordinarily, this would be a fairly worrying signal of potential deceleration. That said, we have to bear in mind that Q3 contained a full month of government shutdown impacts. Though the U.S. is an expansion market for Cognyte, which is primarily centered around European and UK clients, its public sector focus gave it substantial exposure to the stoppage of new deals. That said, we are encouraged by the fact that management noted many discussions have picked right back up after the government reopened. Per CEO Elad Sharon's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Of course, shutdown disrupted the engagement for a certain amount of time. But having said that, it doesn't change the fact that those agencies are facing challenges, new technology. And for that reason, I believe that they'll come back to the table. Some of the federal customers that we were engaging with already came to us after the shutdown relief and asked to resume discussions.

I can also tell you in the U.S. that regardless of the shutdown, we continue to do a lot of efforts in order to expand our market access and brand awareness, we enhanced the sales and marketing activities. We participated in relevant industry conferences that I shared in previous calls, the example is Natia. We are expanding our partners' network. We signed with LexisNexis in Q3. So we have a lot of activities running with federal agencies in the U.S."

On the profitability front, the company also continued to enjoy economies of scale on a growing top line. Adjusted EBITDA grew 81% y/y to $11.9 million, representing an 11.8% margin that improved 440bps y/y.

Key Takeaways

Cognyte is trading at a low-teens forward multiple of adjusted EBITDA despite a >80% y/y growth pace in adjusted EBITDA, backed by double-digit revenue growth and a sticky product dealing in huge vertical markets. In my view, this company is a great way to retain AI exposure in your portfolio while still paying a reasonable price.

data.title: AI Bubble Or Business Revolution? Essential Business Insights
data.description:
"What about the AI bubble?" is the most common question being asked of economists and investment professionals today. Skepticism is appropriate about two issues: the assertion of a bubble, and the valuation of companies in new fields. Bubbles are hard to identify in real time, though underlying demand may be easier to see.

Underlying fundamentals are extremely positive for artificial intelligence companies, with two major economic activities almost certain to prove strong. Companies producing large language models (LLMs) will be a major engine of productivity across a very wide swath of business, non-profits and government. In addition, a multitude of companies are developing specialized applications that use LLMs to improve productivity in specific tasks, such as billing or product design. In addition to these two major approaches, small language models for specialized tasks have been developed, though it's less certain that this approach will beat specialized apps connected to LLMs.

But strong fundamentals do not always justify sky-high company valuations. The trick is to figure out how high the fundamentals will be relative to current valuations. That is incredibly hard to do, except in retrospect.

The AI industry is figuring out the best business model for applying technology to real-world problems. That combines with human tendency toward speculative frenzies to make valuation difficult. The business model challenge has been under-appreciated in recent discussions.

In 1908, the United States had over 250 companies manufacturing automobiles. Two major trends followed: Cars sales soared to previously unbelievable levels, and most car companies went out of business. That's a stunning result.

Car companies had to figure out how to manufacture the product efficiently - something that Ford was good at. And they had to figure out what customers most wanted in their cars, which General Motors was better at. In an industry with large economies of scale, the lower-performing companies lost market share, causing their costs to exceed that of other companies. So they failed.

Similar results came in the dot-com boom of the 1990s. Many companies were formed in e-commerce, and most went out of business. But online shopping has hit levels that few expected two decades ago. The business concept was great, but most of the specific attempts failed.

Underlying this dichotomy is a concept I call "the trial and error economy." Businesses need to experiment even though many experiments will fail - or they succeed in the sense that the company learns what will not work. Rarely does a major innovation work perfectly in its first iteration.

In the AI space, we will watch many errors. Some companies will fail. Other will stumble badly but survive. And a few will find the right model for helping their customers achieve huge gains in productivity - and they will end up being worth their lofty valuations.

Market structure is part of the puzzle as to ultimate company success. Sometimes one company dominates a particular space. Who can name the number two company in small business accounting software? That’s because every accountant can load a computer file created with QuickBooks. Small business owners with an alternative accounting program have more trouble.

Some industries have a handful of big players, while others have many small players - think of restaurants. I wrote a while back: “Whereas the large language model will resemble the Airbus-Boeing oligopoly, the applications sector will look like sushi, burgers, pizza and on and on.” That is, the businesses providing very specific applications will be numerous, using very specific knowledge of a sector. When an electrical contractor wants faster turnaround times for bids, he will turn to an AI product developed with intimate knowledge of that particular niche: estimating costs of electrical installations. That program will not help to the car dealer trying to boost the service department’s sales, but some other app will. However, those application providers may use the same LLM as the engine behind the scene.

As for stock valuation, consider that Amazon (AMZN) stock reached a high in 1999. Two years later, the stock had dropped 95%. It turned out, though, that buying at the 1999 high wasn’t so stupid. The stock is now worth 52 times that old 1999 peak. A comparable investment in the Standard & Poor’s 500 index would, with dividends reinvested, be worth about than eight times the amount invested.

The Amazon story is valuable not only for its eventual monetary value but because of the wild ride along the way. The stock has had plenty of setbacks along its journey. And investors should remember that many dot-com investments became worthless.

Is the AI industry in a bubble? I honestly don’t know. Every individual stock in the sector is pretty risky at current prices, but some will prove to be worth today’s price and more. Many investments will be total losses. However, the evidence is strong that AI will be a huge force in the business world for years to come.

Original Post

data.title: Forget Long-Term Treasuries: Your Portfolio Needs A New Hedge
data.description:
What’s the first thing that comes to your mind when you think about a government bond? Probably a safe investment, but much more important, a way to hedge against market crashes.

When a recession hits (and it will, sooner or later), being invested 100% in stocks might be very painful over the short-to-medium term. There is a big difference between a portfolio that faces a 40-50% decline compared to a correction of 10-20%, but can bonds still act as a hedge against market headwinds?

The inverse correlation no longer holds

First of all, not all bonds are the same. In terms of performance during bear markets, high-yield bonds can be comparable to equity; therefore, they don’t offer enough protection.

But at the same time, they also tend to significantly underperform the S&P 500 Index over the long run. In other words, not a great pick if you want to reduce the riskiness of your portfolio or enhance the long-term performance.

During recessions, people want to buy high-quality assets, and US Treasury Bonds have typically fit this description in the past. However, there is a further aspect to consider: the maturity.

The main goal of investing in bonds is including in the portfolio an asset class whose performance is disconnected from the stock market. But here is the catch: if you buy a short-term bond, the potential capital appreciation is lower than a long-term bond, as the latter presents a higher duration (to put it simply, the price sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations).

So, if a recession hits your stock market portfolio, long-term bonds should protect you more than short-term bonds. Here is a visual explanation that covers the 2002-2014 period.

IEF is an ETF that includes US Treasuries with a maturity included between 7 and 10 years. As you can see, during the Great Financial Crisis, this ETF went in the opposite direction of the stock market, providing some relief from such a negative period. However, the IEF price appreciation wasn’t enough to cover the stock market losses unless you had a huge position in it.

If you wanted to maximize the protection, you should have picked TLT, an ETF that includes Treasury bonds whose maturity is at least 20 years.

Holding TLT in 2008-2009 could have avoided a significant part of your losses, especially if you followed the 60/40 portfolio rule. Until recently, the 60/40 approach showed impressive performances in terms of risk/return across multiple macroeconomic environments, but maybe something is changing.

The 60/40 rule works as long as US Treasuries hold up a negative correlation with the S&P 500; however, this doesn’t seem to be the case anymore.

During the 2022 bear market, TLT collapsed more than the stock market, so bonds didn’t act as a shield against losses. This year, following the Liberation Day, the stock market plummeted, and TLT remained flat. Of course, it's better than falling 20%, but if I want no volatility and some coupons along the way, I’d rather invest in a money market ETF.

The last time TLT worked properly was in 2020, when the pandemic affected the whole world. Investors panicked, and capital has flowed away from risky assets to flood safe assets like US Treasuries. So, what has changed since then? The US creditworthiness.

The pandemic was a turning point in terms of fiscal deficit/GDP: since then, the economy has become addicted to huge fiscal spending, even if there isn’t a recession. The stock market always wants new fiscal stimulus or rate cuts to keep climbing, regardless of what is really needed. This mechanism ensures ample liquidity in the financial system, but at the same time, it is hurting the debt sustainability over the long term. That’s why long-term Treasury bond yields remain high even though the Fed is cutting rates.

Investors don’t feel safe anymore to invest in long-term maturities, or at least they are not as confident as they were about five years ago. When the next recession will occur, there is no guarantee that TLT could offset part of your losses as it did in 2008 and 2020.

Actually, a general distrust in the long-term debt sustainability might only exacerbate your losses. Besides the US creditworthiness, to achieve a positive performance, TLT needs a macroeconomic environment where the economy is heading toward a deflationary phase, quite the opposite of what we are currently experiencing.

I know that TLT looks cheap now, but one shouldn’t overlook the risks related to this ETF. The current economic context presents both conditions (distrust toward long-term maturities and an inflationary environment) to keep it low for much longer. And I personally don’t rule out the possibility of a further downside, especially if the inflation rate spikes up in 2026.

At this point you might think that it is just TLT that is the problem, not individual long-term US Treasuries. After all, the latter has a duration that decreases over time, and you are guaranteed to achieve a nominal profit. If you invest $10,000 in a 30Y US Treasury Bond, you are going to get $476 every year for 30 years, and at maturity you get the par value.

However, what about the performance along the way? If it doesn’t protect your portfolio during market crashes, the usefulness of this investment drops significantly. Furthermore, even if it is considered safe, it doesn’t mean that it is free from any risk.

A clear example of how risky safe bonds can be is the Austria 100y bond. Austria has the highest rating (AAA), but in 5 years the price of the 100Y bond went from €138 to €30, a 78% drop. This is an example of unusual extreme duration, but I think it is very useful to point out the market risk that every long-term bond deals with, even if AAA rated (By the way, the US is no longer AAA).

Now, let’s assume that you don’t care about the market risk, and for whatever reason you want to hold a long-term US Treasury bond for its entire lifespan. You have a 4.76% guaranteed coupon, but is it really worth it? If your investment horizon is so long, it makes much more sense to just invest in the stock market.

If you invest in the S&P 500 and your holding period is 3 years, you have a positive return probability of 84%; if you hold for 5 years, it rises to 88%. If you hold for 10 years, that’s a 94% probability, and if you hold for 20 years, there is no way you won’t make money. Consider that these stats include cherry-picking the worst moment to be invested in the stock market; you are probably not that unlucky.

Overall, US long-term Treasury bonds didn’t protect investors during the last 2 market crashes, and I am doubting they will when the next one occurs. I don’t see how long-term yields can drop without an actual plan to make the debt more sustainable, and since there is none, this could mean prolonged suffering for TLT. So, if you really want to diversify your portfolio, what are the assets you can rely on?

Potential solutions

The perfect solution to diversify your portfolio doesn’t exist, but there are still some options that might help you. With long-term government bonds off the table, the first interesting choice might be investing in a short-term government bond ETF (for example, BIL). Sure, the low constant duration doesn’t allow you to offset most of the potential losses from a market crash, but neither has TLT done so recently. The dividend yield is pretty close (4.14% BIL, 4.36% TLT), but with BIL you eliminate the market risk, and you will have more flexibility.

If inflation spikes up in 2026, you get stuck in TLT until interest rates come down once again, something that won’t happen with BIL. Actually, higher inflation will increase the BIL dividend yield (if, as it should, the Fed Funds Rate gets higher), and you would get every month an increasing amount of cash: very welcome during a market drawdown. I see a higher inflation level than expected as the major risk for 2026; that’s why I consider BIL a valid option. If you want a more tax-efficient strategy, you could pick an accumulating money market ETF.

Clearly, this approach won’t save you from a market crash, but you can’t lose money by investing in BIL, and you would have some dry powder to deploy when market valuations have gone down. In this market phase, I find it wise to accumulate some cash and get risk-free interest out of it.

Berkshire Hathaway seems to agree since the cash pile reached $382 billion, and potentially, adding this company to your portfolio might be a way to hedge against market headwinds. They know how to deal with a stressful period for markets, and they definitely have the potential to benefit from it. Imagine having $400 billion to invest when valuations are cheap.

The third option might be buying gold. This is not an asset that I personally hold since it doesn’t produce cash, but I can’t deny that it worked pretty well during past recessions. And yes, it sounds scary to buy gold after more than doubling its price over the last 2 years, but remember that gold has no fair value and central banks keep increasing their stakes. Consider that, in the period that goes from Aug 1976 to January 1980, gold prices registered almost an 8x. The main trigger for such a strong ascent was a double-digit inflation rate.

The fourth option might be investing in all those companies that have been overlooked because of little involvement in the AI trend. I am talking primarily about consumer staples, companies able to deliver constant and growing profits/dividends. During the Internet bubble, some of them delivered an opposite performance compared to the overall stock market.

These three companies belong to different industries, and they all achieved impressive returns during one of the worst periods for the S&P 500. Therefore, even if the market drops 40%, it doesn’t mean that all the companies experience a sharp drop. Choose carefully and avoid the hype.

Finally, a brief discussion about Bitcoin, sometimes referred to as digital gold.

Well, as you can see from this chart, Bitcoin performance is strictly correlated to that of the Nasdaq-100, so it is quite the opposite of a safe asset. Many analysts believe it could somewhat protect you against a recession, but I consider it just a Nasdaq with a 3x leverage. This is probably not an asset that you want to have during market headwinds.

data.title: Legacy Housing Corporation: Future Growth Prospects Are Compelling
data.description:
The last few months have been downright brutal for shareholders of Legacy Housing Corporation (LEGH). This is a fascinating company, with a focus on the production and sale of affordable tiny and manufactured homes. This is a market that I view in a favorable light, even during these tough times. You would think that, as some of the lowest-end housing opportunities available, during times of inflationary pain and persistently high-interest rates, these types of homes would see a big decline in demand. But at the end of the day, people need housing. And all this does, at least in my opinion, is push people who would normally buy traditional homes down the ladder to purchase these homes. So unless economic conditions get horrible, I would expect this kind of business to perform well.

It was with this line of thinking, as well as its relatively decent valuation, that I reaffirmed my "Buy" rating on the stock in early September of this year. Leading up to that point, the stock had been up significantly more than the broader market. But since then, something has changed. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have taken a hit. Although the average net revenue per unit has increased, the number of units sold by the company declined. This is even though the company continues to focus on some key growth opportunities. And honestly, it does weaken my previous thesis regarding demand for units in this space. Even though the stock has fallen 28% since then, which has been far worse than the 4.8% rise that the S&P 500 saw, there are reasons to be optimistic. Even with this recent weakness, shares of the company are cheap, and it boasts a net cash position as opposed to a net debt position. If anything, I do view this decline that the stock experienced as a buying opportunity.

Still A Good Home For Your Money

The only new data that we currently have regarding Legacy Housing covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, investors did see some pain. Revenue came in at $40.5 million, which was down from the $44.3 million the company reported a year earlier. This drop came even though the average price of a unit delivered rose from $63,500 to $68,500. If these prices seem low, that's because they are. Historically speaking, tiny homes and manufactured homes are incredibly cheap to build. They also tend to be smaller. Admittedly, some of the homes that Legacy Housing sells are up to 2,777 square feet. But many of them are as small as 395 square feet.

The problem for the company ended up being a decline in the number of homes sold. The number of units dropped from 475 to 420. On the pricing side, management benefited from a larger share of company-owned retail units being sold. Price hikes caused by tariffs and inflationary pressures also played a role here. Unfortunately, however, the lower end of the spectrum proved to be problematic for the company. This involved mobile Home Park demand, which dropped. Park operators are buying fewer new units because home, setup, and utility hookup costs are up significantly. But because of pressures on these low-end buyers, low rents have not been able to adjust higher by as much. It also didn't help that fewer units were being sold to independent dealers. And this is because a lot of those dealers are, in management's own words, "aging out." Naturally, the higher pricing turned out to be problematic. And management said that the average increase in the cost per home associated solely with tariffs amounted to roughly $1,200.

The decline in revenue for the company brought with it a decline in profits as well. Net income plummeted from $15.8 million to $8.6 million. Operating cash flow dropped from $13.8 million to $7.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $12.2 million to $9.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $15.8 million to $10.1 million. I already mentioned higher prices, including those associated with tariffs. But management also mentioned another pain point for the company. They acknowledged, for instance, that they have already started to feel the effects of enforcement from ICE as it cracks down on undocumented workers. Estimates vary, but one source figures that between 15% and 23% of all construction workers in the US are undocumented. Admittedly, management has said that this has not put a big strain on the company as of yet. But it has made its effects known.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Because of the weak third quarter, revenue for this period is down. Even though the company saw an increase in the average net revenue per unit from $60,300 to $68,600, the number of units sold dropped sharply from 1,536 to 1,334. Net profits and cash flows fell across the board as well. Despite these problems, a very successful September for its Fort Worth assets indicated that strong demand will exist for the final quarter of 2024 and into the first quarter of 2026. The company also believes that recent acquisitions, such as of AmeriCasa, FutureHomeX, and its Houston lot, will permit between 50% and 60% more retail units next year, maybe even more if things go well, compared to 2025 in its entirety.

For context, management agreed at the end of October to acquire AmeriCasa in a deal valued at $12 million cash as well as certain other consideration in the form of assumed liabilities. Meanwhile, its purchase of FutureHomeX brings into the fold a cloud-based software as a service platform that helps manufactured home retailers and communities. This should certainly draw in some additional revenue. And when it comes to the law in Houston, that purchase could add 100 homes on its own. So at the end of the day, the company remains growth-oriented. And considering that it has no debt on its books and enjoys a cash position of $13.6 million, it certainly has the fuel with which to grow.

If we project our results based on what we have seen already for the year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. The 2025 trading multiples are based on the assumption that net profits will be $43.9 million, that adjusted operating cash flow will be $45.9 million, and that EBITDA will total $52.1 million. In addition to being cheap on an absolute basis, the stock is also attractively priced compared to other home builders, most of whom don't play in the tiny home and manufactured space. In the table below, you can see precisely what I mean. Across the board, only one of these five enterprises is cheaper than our candidate.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Legacy Housing Corporation 10.7 10.2 8.8
Hovnanian Enterprises (HOV) 6.5 6.7 8.2
Beazer Homes USA (BZH) 15.0 21.6 27.3
LGI Homes (LGIH) 11.6 117.7 26.3
Smith-Midland Corporation (SMID) 15.4 19.3 15.4
Cavco Industries (CVCO) 25.0 23.4 17.8

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I know that the recent data provided by management was perhaps rather jarring. It certainly throws into question just how strong my previous assumption was regarding how resilient this industry would be compared to the traditional home building market. Having said that, management remains focused on future growth. Certain issues might affect bottom-line results, such as those involving tariffs, high-interest rates, immigration enforcement, and more. But given how solid the company is from a fundamental standpoint, I don't mind maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate.
